options.text: Muscle weakness only
Excess bone growth
Decreased bone density and increased fracture risk
Joint inflammation only

quiz_questions.explanation: Osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease in which bone resorption exceeds bone formation, resulting in reduced bone mass and density. This makes bones fragile and significantly increases the risk of fractures.

quiz_questions.question_text: The population most at risk for osteoporosis is:

options.text: Teenagers
Children
Young men
Postmenopausal women

quiz_questions.explanation: Postmenopausal women are at highest risk for osteoporosis because the dramatic decline in estrogen levels after menopause removes the protective effect estrogen has on bone, accelerating osteoclast activity and bone loss.

options.text: Caused by mechanical wear and tear
Caused by bone fractures
Found only in the elderly
An autoimmune disease attacking the synovial membrane

quiz_questions.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disease in which the immune system mistakenly attacks the synovial membrane of joints, causing inflammation, swelling, pain, and eventual joint destruction. It can affect people of any age.

options.text: The broken bone has pierced through the skin
Only a hairline crack is present
The bone has bent without breaking completely
The skin remains intact over the fracture

quiz_questions.explanation: A compound (open) fracture is one in which the broken bone has penetrated through the skin, creating an open wound. This type is particularly dangerous because the exposed bone is at high risk for infection.

options.text: A complete break through the bone
An incomplete break common in children where the bone bends
A fracture caused by a twisting force
A fracture that penetrates the skin

quiz_questions.explanation: A greenstick fracture is an incomplete fracture common in children, whose bones are more flexible. Like a green twig, the bone bends and cracks on one side but does not break completely through.

options.text: Bacterial infection of bone
Vitamin D deficiency
Aging and wear
A genetic defect in collagen production

quiz_questions.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta ('brittle bone disease') is a genetic disorder caused by defective type I collagen production. Since collagen provides the framework for bone mineralization, defective collagen results in extremely brittle bones that fracture easily.

options.text: Trauma
Bacterial infection
Uric acid crystal deposits in joints
Cartilage wear and tear

quiz_questions.explanation: Gout is an inflammatory arthritis caused by the deposition of monosodium urate (uric acid) crystals in joints, most commonly the big toe. It results from elevated blood uric acid levels and causes severe, sudden attacks of pain and swelling.

quiz_questions.question_text: DEXA scans are primarily used to diagnose:

options.text: Muscle injuries
Bone fractures
Rheumatoid arthritis
Osteoporosis by measuring bone mineral density

quiz_questions.explanation: Dual-energy X-ray Absorptiometry (DEXA) is the gold standard imaging test for measuring bone mineral density (BMD). It is used to diagnose osteoporosis and osteopenia and to monitor treatment effectiveness.

options.text: Only muscle weakness
Excessive and disorganized bone remodeling
Vitamin D excess
No bone activity at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Paget's disease is characterized by chaotic, excessive bone remodeling in which osteoclasts become overactive, followed by disorganized osteoblast activity. The resulting bone is structurally abnormal, enlarged, and weak despite appearing dense on X-ray.

options.text: A fracture of the vertebral body
A joint dislocation in the spine
A torn spinal ligament only
The nucleus pulposus protruding through the annulus fibrosus

quiz_questions.explanation: A herniated (slipped) disc occurs when the gel-like nucleus pulposus of an intervertebral disc pushes through a tear in the tough outer annulus fibrosus. The protruding material can compress nearby spinal nerves, causing pain, numbness, or weakness.

options.text: A degenerative joint disease caused by wear and tear of articular cartilage
An autoimmune disease that attacks the synovial membrane
An infection of the bone and joint
A metabolic bone disease causing low bone density

quiz_questions.explanation: Osteoarthritis (OA) is the most common form of arthritis, caused by progressive degeneration of articular cartilage from mechanical wear and tear over time. As cartilage erodes, bones rub together, causing pain, stiffness, and loss of mobility.

options.text: A fracture that occurs during surgical procedures
A fracture caused by high-impact trauma in healthy bone
A fracture that occurs in bone weakened by disease, often from minimal trauma
A fracture that fails to heal and remains permanently broken

quiz_questions.explanation: A pathological fracture occurs in bone that has been weakened by an underlying disease such as osteoporosis, bone cancer, or infection. These fractures can occur from minimal force or even normal daily activities that would not break healthy bone.

options.text: A fracture caused by bone disease
A small crack in bone caused by repetitive, low-impact forces over time
A fracture caused by a single traumatic impact
A fracture that penetrates through the skin

quiz_questions.explanation: A stress fracture is a small crack in a bone that develops gradually from repetitive, submaximal forces that exceed the bone's ability to repair microdamage. They are common in runners, military recruits, and athletes who rapidly increase training intensity.

options.text: An injury in which bones at a joint are forced out of their normal alignment
A break in the continuity of a bone
Stretching or tearing of a tendon
Inflammation of the bursa surrounding a joint

quiz_questions.explanation: A dislocation (luxation) occurs when the articular surfaces of bones at a joint are completely displaced from their normal position. It typically involves stretching or tearing of the joint capsule and ligaments and requires medical reduction to realign the bones.

options.text: An abnormal lateral curvature of the spine
A normal developmental curvature of the spine
An excessive anterior curvature of the lumbar spine
An excessive posterior curvature of the thoracic spine

quiz_questions.explanation: Scoliosis is an abnormal lateral (side-to-side) curvature of the spine, typically presenting as a C-shaped or S-shaped deviation. Most cases are idiopathic (unknown cause) and are typically diagnosed during adolescence.

options.text: An excessive inward lumbar curve
A fracture of multiple vertebrae
An excessive posterior curvature of the thoracic spine, commonly called hunchback
An abnormal lateral curvature of the spine

quiz_questions.explanation: Kyphosis is an exaggerated posterior convexity of the thoracic spine, producing a rounded upper back or 'hunchback' appearance. It can result from poor posture, osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures, or degenerative disc disease.

options.text: An excessive posterior curvature of the upper back
An excessive anterior curvature of the lumbar spine, commonly called swayback
Compression of vertebral bodies
A lateral curvature of the spine

quiz_questions.explanation: Lordosis is an exaggerated inward (anterior) curvature of the lumbar spine, often called swayback. Contributing factors include obesity, pregnancy, poor posture, and weak abdominal muscles that fail to adequately support the lower spine.

options.text: Softening of bones due to vitamin deficiency
Excessive bone remodeling
A benign tumor of bone tissue
An infection of the bone and bone marrow, usually caused by bacteria

quiz_questions.explanation: Osteomyelitis is an infection of bone and bone marrow, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus bacteria. It can reach bone through the bloodstream, direct contamination from an open fracture, or spread from an adjacent soft tissue infection.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term comminuted fracture mean?

options.text: A fracture where the skin is pierced by the bone
A fracture along the length of the bone
A fracture where the bone bends without breaking completely
A fracture in which the bone is shattered into three or more fragments

quiz_questions.explanation: A comminuted fracture is one in which the bone is broken into three or more pieces (fragments). These fractures typically result from high-energy trauma and are more difficult to treat because the multiple fragments must be realigned and stabilized.

options.text: Medications that inhibit osteoclast activity, used to treat osteoporosis
Antibiotics used to treat bone infections
Hormones that stimulate bone growth in children
Minerals that strengthen bone directly

quiz_questions.explanation: Bisphosphonates (such as alendronate and risedronate) are a class of medications that slow bone loss by inhibiting osteoclast activity. They are widely used as first-line treatment for osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 72-year-old woman stumbles on a curb and falls onto her hip. X-rays reveal a fracture of the femoral neck even though the fall seemed minor. Her physician explains that the fracture occurred because her bones were already weakened. What type of fracture is this?

options.text: A stress fracture from repetitive activity
A pathological fracture due to osteoporosis
A greenstick fracture
A compound (open) fracture

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a pathological fracture because the bone was already weakened by osteoporosis before the fall occurred. Minimal trauma that would not fracture healthy bone can cause breaks in osteoporotic bone, especially at the femoral neck, vertebrae, and distal radius.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 45-year-old woman reports symmetric swelling, warmth, and morning stiffness in the small joints of both hands. Blood tests reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the underlying mechanism?

options.text: Rheumatoid arthritis, an autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks the synovial membrane
Osteoarthritis caused by mechanical wear of the cartilage
Osteoporosis causing bone pain in the hands
Gout caused by uric acid crystal deposition

quiz_questions.explanation: The symmetric joint involvement, morning stiffness, and positive autoimmune markers (rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP) are classic for rheumatoid arthritis. RA is an autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks the synovial membrane, causing chronic inflammation and progressive joint destruction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marathon runner develops gradually worsening pain in her right shin over several weeks of increasing training mileage. Imaging reveals a small crack in the tibia without any history of acute trauma. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Osteomyelitis
A compound fracture
A stress fracture
Paget's disease

quiz_questions.explanation: A stress fracture develops from repetitive, submaximal mechanical loading that overwhelms the bone's ability to repair microdamage. The gradual onset of pain during increased training, without a single traumatic event, is the hallmark presentation of a stress fracture.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with a known history of heavy alcohol use and a diet rich in red meat and shellfish wakes up at night with excruciating pain, redness, and swelling in his big toe. His physician diagnoses gout. What is the underlying cause of this patient's joint inflammation?

options.text: Autoimmune destruction of the synovial membrane
Degeneration of articular cartilage from overuse
Bacterial infection from contaminated food
Deposition of monosodium urate (uric acid) crystals in the joint, triggering an intense inflammatory response

quiz_questions.explanation: Gout is caused by hyperuricemia (elevated blood uric acid) leading to the deposition of monosodium urate crystals in joints. These sharp crystals trigger an intense inflammatory response, causing severe pain and swelling, most commonly in the first metatarsophalangeal joint (big toe).

quiz_questions.question_text: A child is brought to the hospital after multiple fractures from seemingly minor falls. The child also has blue-tinged sclera (whites of the eyes). Genetic testing confirms a collagen synthesis defect. What condition does this child most likely have?

options.text: Rickets
Paget's disease
Osteogenesis imperfecta
Osteoporosis

quiz_questions.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) is a genetic disorder causing defective type I collagen production, resulting in extremely brittle bones that fracture easily. Blue sclera is a classic associated finding because the thin, collagen-deficient sclera allows the underlying blood vessels to show through.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 55-year-old man presents with lower back pain radiating down his left leg. MRI shows that the nucleus pulposus of an intervertebral disc has herniated posterolaterally and is compressing the L5 nerve root. Why does a herniated disc cause leg pain?

options.text: The herniated material fractures the vertebral body
The protruding nucleus pulposus compresses the spinal nerve root, causing pain, numbness, or weakness in the area the nerve supplies
The disc material enters the bloodstream and causes inflammation in the leg
The annulus fibrosus tears the surrounding ligaments

quiz_questions.explanation: When the nucleus pulposus herniates through the annulus fibrosus, it can compress adjacent spinal nerve roots. Since these nerves travel to specific body regions, compression causes radiating pain, numbness, or weakness along the nerve's distribution, commonly down the leg (sciatica).

quiz_questions.question_text: A 10-year-old boy falls from a tree and presents with a forearm that is bent but the X-ray shows the bone is only cracked on one side. The emergency physician calls it a specific type of fracture common in children. What type of fracture is this?

options.text: A compound fracture
A spiral fracture
A comminuted fracture
A greenstick fracture

quiz_questions.explanation: A greenstick fracture is an incomplete break that occurs almost exclusively in children because their bones contain more collagen and are more flexible than adult bones. Like a green twig, the bone bends and cracks on the tension side but does not break completely.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with an open (compound) tibial fracture arrives at the emergency department. The orthopedic surgeon is especially concerned about a complication that is not a risk with closed fractures. What complication is the surgeon most worried about?

options.text: Bone infection (osteomyelitis) because the wound exposes the bone to bacteria
Premature growth plate closure
Excessive callus formation
Delayed healing from poor blood supply

quiz_questions.explanation: In an open (compound) fracture, the bone penetrates through the skin, creating a direct pathway for bacteria to enter the wound and reach the bone. This dramatically increases the risk of osteomyelitis (bone infection), which is why aggressive wound cleaning and antibiotics are critical.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physical education teacher screens adolescent students for spinal abnormalities. She asks each student to bend forward and checks for a rib hump or asymmetric trunk contour. What condition is she screening for, and why is early detection important?

options.text: Scoliosis; early detection allows for bracing or intervention before the curvature worsens during the growth spurt
Osteoporosis; early detection prevents future fractures
Kyphosis; early detection prevents rib fractures
Lordosis; early detection prevents lower back pain

quiz_questions.explanation: The forward bend (Adams) test screens for scoliosis by revealing trunk asymmetry or a rib hump caused by vertebral rotation. Early detection during adolescence is critical because intervention (such as bracing) can prevent the curvature from worsening during the growth spurt.

quiz_questions.question_text: An elderly man is found to have thickened, deformed bones in his skull and pelvis on X-ray, with markedly elevated serum alkaline phosphatase levels. His physician diagnoses him with a disorder of excessive, disorganized bone remodeling. What is this condition?

options.text: Paget's disease of bone
Rickets
Osteogenesis imperfecta
Osteoporosis

quiz_questions.explanation: Paget's disease of bone is characterized by chaotic, excessive bone remodeling, producing thickened, enlarged, but structurally weak bone. Elevated alkaline phosphatase reflects the intense osteoblast activity. It most commonly affects the pelvis, skull, spine, and long bones of the legs.
